Robust approaches to modelling fragile conservation biology: flexible solutions to non-normal, hierarchical, temporal, spatial, and phylogenetic data

Yefeng Yang, Jinming Pan, David Warton, Shinichi Nakagawa

10-03-2026

0 Code attribution

Parts of the diagnostic workflow and example code in this document are inspired by or adapted from:

Hartig, F. Advanced Regression Models with R.

This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 4.0 International License (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0).

The examples provided here modify and extend the original material by using simulated datasets and workflows tailored to demonstrate (temporal) autocorrelation, heteroscedasticity, and outliers diagnostics.

1 Simulate temporally autocorrelated ecological data

We simulate species richness driven by temperature, with AR(1) temporal dependence.

set.seed(2026)

AR1sim <- function(n, phi, sigma = 1){
  x <- numeric(n)
  x[1] <- rnorm(1,0,sigma)
  for(i in 2:n){
    x[i] <- phi * x[i-1] + rnorm(1,0,sigma)
  }
  return(x)
}

n <- 1000
year <- 1:n

temperature <- scale(rnorm(n, 15, 3))

#  temporal autocorrelation
epsilon <- AR1sim(n, phi = 0.9, sigma = 0.5)

richness <- 20 + 2*temperature + epsilon

temporal_data <- data.frame(
  year = year,
  temperature = as.numeric(temperature),
  richness = richness
)

plot(temporal_data$year, temporal_data$richness, type="l")

2 Fit a model ignoring the structured ecological trend

First, let’s include any predictors and thus we do not account for any temporal trend.

ignore_trend = lm(richness ~ 1, data = temporal_data)
summary(ignore_trend)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = richness ~ 1, data = temporal_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -8.8622 -1.5247  0.0568  1.4462  7.2554 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 19.92457    0.07224   275.8   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 2.284 on 999 degrees of freedom

Check the residuals:

plot(residuals(ignore_trend))

Have a look at the correlation between richness as a function of their lag (temporal distance):

library(DHARMa)
## This is DHARMa 0.4.7. For overview type '?DHARMa'. For recent changes, type news(package = 'DHARMa')
acf(residuals(ignore_trend), type = "correlation", plot = T)

pacf(residuals(ignore_trend), plot = T)

testTemporalAutocorrelation(ignore_trend, time = temporal_data$year)

## 
##  Durbin-Watson test
## 
## data:  simulationOutput$scaledResiduals ~ 1
## DW = 1.6459, p-value = 2.097e-08
## alternative hypothesis: true autocorrelation is not 0

3 Fit a model capturing the ecological trend

This model accounts for temporal trend but does not model temporal dependence.

mod_trend = lm(richness ~ temperature, data = temporal_data)
summary(mod_trend)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = richness ~ temperature, data = temporal_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.7551 -0.7502 -0.0138  0.8451  3.2166 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 19.92457    0.03576  557.19   <2e-16 ***
## temperature  1.98520    0.03578   55.49   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 1.131 on 998 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.7552, Adjusted R-squared:  0.755 
## F-statistic:  3079 on 1 and 998 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Fit a model accounting for temporal dependence, but not temporal trend:

library(nlme)
mod_trend2 <- gls(
  richness ~ temperature,
  data = temporal_data
)
summary(mod_trend2)
## Generalized least squares fit by REML
##   Model: richness ~ temperature 
##   Data: temporal_data 
##        AIC    BIC    logLik
##   3097.383 3112.1 -1545.692
## 
## Coefficients:
##                 Value  Std.Error  t-value p-value
## (Intercept) 19.924571 0.03575918 557.1875       0
## temperature  1.985204 0.03577708  55.4882       0
## 
##  Correlation: 
##             (Intr)
## temperature 0     
## 
## Standardized residuals:
##         Min          Q1         Med          Q3         Max 
## -3.32075490 -0.66340322 -0.01220586  0.74737457  2.84455330 
## 
## Residual standard error: 1.130805 
## Degrees of freedom: 1000 total; 998 residual

Given that gls is not a DHARMa object, we now use glmmTMB for illustration:

library(glmmTMB)
## Warning: package 'glmmTMB' was built under R version 4.0.5
## Warning in checkDepPackageVersion(dep_pkg = "TMB"): Package version inconsistency detected.
## glmmTMB was built with TMB version 1.8.1
## Current TMB version is 1.9.14
## Please re-install glmmTMB from source or restore original 'TMB' package (see '?reinstalling' for more information)
mod_trend3 = glmmTMB(richness ~ ar1(factor(year) + 0 | 1), data = temporal_data) # `1` can be replaced by `grp` indicating multiple time series
summary(mod_trend3)
##  Family: gaussian  ( identity )
## Formula:          richness ~ ar1(factor(year) + 0 | 1)
## Data: temporal_data
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##   4493.1   4502.9  -2244.5   4489.1      998 
## 
## 
## Dispersion estimate for gaussian family (sigma^2): 5.21 
## 
## Conditional model:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)  19.9246     0.0722   275.9   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
testTemporalAutocorrelation(mod_trend3, time = temporal_data$year)

## 
##  Durbin-Watson test
## 
## data:  simulationOutput$scaledResiduals ~ 1
## DW = 1.646, p-value = 2.107e-08
## alternative hypothesis: true autocorrelation is not 0

Fit a model accounting for both temporal trend and temporal dependence:

# using gls
mod_trend4 <- gls(
  richness ~ temperature, 
  corr = corAR1(0.9, form = ~ 1),
  data = temporal_data
)
summary(mod_trend4)
## Generalized least squares fit by REML
##   Model: richness ~ temperature 
##   Data: temporal_data 
##        AIC     BIC    logLik
##   1399.057 1418.68 -695.5287
## 
## Correlation Structure: AR(1)
##  Formula: ~1 
##  Parameter estimate(s):
##       Phi 
## 0.9055215 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                 Value Std.Error  t-value p-value
## (Intercept) 19.928897 0.1601353 124.4504       0
## temperature  1.988704 0.0112399 176.9326       0
## 
##  Correlation: 
##             (Intr)
## temperature -0.002
## 
## Standardized residuals:
##         Min          Q1         Med          Q3         Max 
## -3.30588263 -0.66366522 -0.01672808  0.74095528  2.81994165 
## 
## Residual standard error: 1.138332 
## Degrees of freedom: 1000 total; 998 residual
#using glmmTMB
mod_trend5 = glmmTMB(richness ~ temperature + ar1(0 + factor(year) | 1), data = temporal_data)
summary(mod_trend5)
##  Family: gaussian  ( identity )
## Formula:          richness ~ temperature + ar1(0 + factor(year) | 1)
## Data: temporal_data
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##   3087.7   3102.5  -1540.9   3081.7      997 
## 
## 
## Dispersion estimate for gaussian family (sigma^2): 1.28 
## 
## Conditional model:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept) 19.92457    0.03572   557.7   <2e-16 ***
## temperature  1.98520    0.03574    55.5   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

4 Residual diagnostics with DHARMa

The diagnostic plots evaluate whether residuals behave as expected under the fitted model:

We look for

  • uniform residual distribution

  • no strong structure in residual plots

  • no systematic trends

If temporal autocorrelation exists, residuals often show structured deviations from randomness.

For the diagnostic of the model accounting for temporal trend but does not model temporal dependence (mod_trend):

acf(residuals(mod_trend), type = "correlation", plot = T)

pacf(residuals(mod_trend), plot = T)

library(DHARMa)
res <- simulateResiduals(mod_trend)
plot(res)
## Warning in newton(lsp = lsp, X = G$X, y = G$y, Eb = G$Eb, UrS = G$UrS, L =
## G$L, : Fitting terminated with step failure - check results carefully

testTemporalAutocorrelation(res, time = temporal_data$year)

## 
##  Durbin-Watson test
## 
## data:  simulationOutput$scaledResiduals ~ 1
## DW = 0.21232, p-value < 2.2e-16
## alternative hypothesis: true autocorrelation is not 0

For the diagnostic of the model accounting for temporal dependence but does not model temporal trend (mod_trend3):

acf(residuals(mod_trend3), type = "correlation", plot = T)

pacf(residuals(mod_trend3), plot = T)

res <- simulateResiduals(mod_trend3)
plot(res)

testTemporalAutocorrelation(res, time = temporal_data$year)

## 
##  Durbin-Watson test
## 
## data:  simulationOutput$scaledResiduals ~ 1
## DW = 1.646, p-value = 2.107e-08
## alternative hypothesis: true autocorrelation is not 0

For the diagnostic of the model accounting for both temporal dependence temporal trend (mod_trend3):

acf(residuals(mod_trend5), type = "correlation", plot = T)

pacf(residuals(mod_trend5), plot = T)

res <- simulateResiduals(mod_trend5)
plot(res)

testTemporalAutocorrelation(res, time = temporal_data$year)

## 
##  Durbin-Watson test
## 
## data:  simulationOutput$scaledResiduals ~ 1
## DW = 0.21245, p-value < 2.2e-16
## alternative hypothesis: true autocorrelation is not 0

Important interpretation principle:

We do not recommend relying solely on the P-value obtained from the DHARMa package. We encourage evaluate:

  • magnitude of the autocorrelation statistic

  • residual plots

  • ecological plausibility

5 Diagnosing heteroscedasticity

Let’s visually and statistically diagonise potential heteroscedasticity of the fitted model:

# visual
plotResiduals(res, temporal_data$temperature)

# statistical
testDispersion(res)

## 
##  DHARMa nonparametric dispersion test via sd of residuals fitted vs.
##  simulated
## 
## data:  simulationOutput
## dispersion = 0.99741, p-value = 0.952
## alternative hypothesis: two.sided

6 Detecting outliers

Let’s visually and statistically diagonise potential outliers of the fitted model:

# visual
plot(res, quantreg = TRUE)

# statistical
testOutliers(res)

## 
##  DHARMa outlier test based on exact binomial test with approximate
##  expectations
## 
## data:  res
## outliers at both margin(s) = 6, observations = 1000, p-value = 0.5952
## alternative hypothesis: true probability of success is not equal to 0.007968127
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.002204982 0.013013423
## sample estimates:
## frequency of outliers (expected: 0.00796812749003984 ) 
##                                                  0.006

Declaration

This document contains adapted material inspired by examples from Florian Hartig’s Advanced Regression Models with R.

Licensed under Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 4.0. Adaptations in this document include new simulated datasets (completely different data-generation process), modified code examples, and integration with DHARMa-based diagnostics.

Package information

subset(data.frame(sessioninfo::package_info()), attached==TRUE, c(package, loadedversion))
##         package loadedversion
## DHARMa   DHARMa         0.4.7
## glmmTMB glmmTMB         1.1.3
## nlme       nlme       3.1-151
